annotator S.report_text:
The actor often spearphishes targets with e-mails containing a link to a hacked website. Adversaries may send spearphishing emails with a malicious link in an attempt to gain access to victim systems.

Sometimes it is a high profile, legitimate site such as "diplomacy.pl", hosting a ZIP archive. The ZIP archive contains a RAR SFX which installs the malware and shows an empty PDF decoy. Adversaries may compromise third-party infrastructure.

Adversaries may transfer tools or other files from an external system into a compromised environment.

In other highly successful runs, this actor sends out phony flash videos directly as email attachments Adversaries may send spearphishing emails with a malicious attachment in an attempt to gain access to victim systems.

The executable within not only plays a flash video, but drops and runs another CozyDuke executable. These videos are quickly passed around offices with delight while systems are infected in the background silently An adversary may rely upon a user clicking a malicious file in order to gain execution.

It first launches Monkeys.exe, playing a selfcontained, very funny video of white-collar tie wearing chimpanzees working in a high rise office with a human colleague. An adversary may rely upon a user clicking a malicious file in order to gain execution.

The file collects system information An adversary may attempt to get detailed information about the operating system and hardware, including version, patches, hotfixes, service packs, and architecture.

invokes a WMI instance in the rootsecuritycenter namespace to identify security products installed on the system Adversaries may abuse Windows Management Instrumentation (WMI) to execute malicious commands and payloads.

It then launches player.exe, a CozyDuke dropper maintaining anti-detection techniques: The player.exe dropper uses anti-detection techniques to avoid detection.

In addition to the WMI/wql use, it also hunts through the "SOFTWAREMicrosoftWindowsCurrentVersionUninstall" registry key looking for security products to avoid. Adversaries may interact with the Windows Registry to gather information about the system, configuration, and installed software.

it drops several more malware files signed with the pasted AMD digital signature to a directory it creates Adversaries may bypass process and/or signature-based defenses by proxying execution of malicious content with signed, or otherwise trusted, binaries.

These files are stored within an 217kb encrypted cab file in the dropper's resources under the name "A". The cab file was encrypted and decrypted using a simple xor cipher with a rotating 16 byte key Adversaries may attempt to make an executable or file difficult to discover or analyze by encrypting, encoding, or otherwise obfuscating its contents on the system or in transit.

Adversaries may use Obfuscated Files or Information to hide artifacts of an intrusion from analysis. They may require separate mechanisms to decode or deobfuscate that information depending on how they intend to use it.

The code copies rundll32.exe from windowssystem32 to its newly created %appdata%ATI_Subsystem subdirectory as "amdocl_as32.exe" alongside the three dll's listed above. It runs atiumdag.dll with two parameter values, it's only export and an arbitrary pid Adversaries may attempt to manipulate features of their artifacts to make them appear legitimate or benign to users and/or security tools.

Adversaries may execute their own malicious payloads by side-loading DLLs.

The code collects information about the system and xml formats this data prior to encryption for proper parsing: An adversary may attempt to get detailed information about the operating system and hardware, including version, patches, hotfixes, service packs, and architecture.

Finally, this process beacons to www.sanjosemaristas.com Adversaries may communicate using OSI application layer protocols to avoid detection/network filtering by blending in with existing traffic.

It uses standard Win32 base cryptography functions to generate a CALG_RC4 session key to encrypt the collected data communications and POSTs it to the server. Adversaries may employ an encryption algorithm to conceal command and control traffic rather than relying on any inherent protections provided by a communication protocol.

Samples are usually signed with a fake certificate – we've seen two instances, one AMD and one Intel Adversaries may bypass process and/or signature-based defenses by proxying execution of malicious content with signed, or otherwise trusted, binaries.

Some of the malware uses an encrypted configuration file which is stored on disk as "racss.dat". This is encrypted by RC4, using the key {0xb5, 0x78, 0x62, 0x52, 0x98, 0x3e, 0x24, 0xd7, 0x3b, 0xc6, 0xee, 0x7c, 0xb9, 0xed, 0x91, 0x62}. Adversaries may attempt to make an executable or file difficult to discover or analyze by encrypting, encoding, or otherwise obfuscating its contents on the system or in transit.

The C&C scripts store these temporarily until the victim next connects to retrieve local files. C&Cs temporarily store commands and new forms until the victim reconnects to retrieve local files.

The attackers send commands and new modules to be executed to the victims through the C&Cs. Adversaries may transfer tools or other files from an external system into a compromised environment.

These are BASE64 encoded and use the same RC4 encryption key as the malware configuration. Adversaries may attempt to make an executable or file difficult to discover or analyze by encrypting, encoding, or otherwise obfuscating its contents on the system or in transit.

screenshot_task.dll is a 32-bit dll used to take a screenshot of the full desktop window and save it as a bitmap in %temp%. Adversaries may attempt to take screen captures of the desktop to gather information over the course of an operation.

cmd_task.dll is a 32-bit dll that maintains several primitives. It is used to create new processes, perform as a command line shell, and several other tasks. Adversaries may abuse command and script interpreters to execute commands, scripts, or binaries.

Structurally, "Cache.dll" is a fairly large backdoor at 425KB. It maintains both code and data in the raw, encrypted blobs of data to be decrypted and used at runtime, and hidden functionality that isn't exposed until runtime. Adversaries may abuse command and script interpreters to execute commands, scripts, or binaries.

Adversaries may attempt to make an executable or file difficult to discover or analyze by encrypting, encoding, or otherwise obfuscating its contents on the system or in transit.

It first grabs an encrypted blob stored away in a global variable and pulls out 381 bytes of this encrypted data Adversaries may attempt to make an executable or file difficult to discover or analyze by encrypting, encoding, or otherwise obfuscating its contents on the system or in transit.

The standard win32 api CryptDecrypt uses rc4 to decrypt this blob into a hardcoded c2, url path, and url parameters listed below with a simple 140-bit key Adversaries may use Obfuscated Files or Information to hide artifacts of an intrusion from analysis. They may require separate mechanisms to decode or deobfuscate that information depending on how they intend to use it.

Much like the prior office monkey "atiumdag.dll" component, this code collects identifying system information using standard win32 API calls An adversary may attempt to get detailed information about the operating system and hardware, including version, patches, hotfixes, service packs, and architecture.

It then uses the runtime resolved networking API calls to send the collected data back to a hardcoded c2 and set of urls. Adversaries may communicate using OSI application layer protocols to avoid detection/network filtering by blending in with existing traffic.

Adversaries may steal data by exfiltrating it over an existing command and control channel.

annotator L.report_text:
CozyDuke

The actor often spearphishes targets with e-mails containing a link to a hacked website.
Sometimes it is a high profile, legitimate site such as "diplomacy.pl", hosting a ZIP archive.
The ZIP archive contains a RAR SFX which installs the malware.
this actor sends out phony flash videos directly as email attachments.
The executable within not only plays a flash video, but drops and runs another CozyDuke executable.
It first launches Monkeys.exe, playing a selfcontained, very funny video of white-collar tie wearing chimpanzees working in a high rise office with a human colleague.
a CozyDuke dropper maintaining anti-detection techniques.
The file collects system information,
then invokes a WMI instance in the rootsecuritycenter namespace to identify security products installed on the system.
In addition to the WMI/wql use, it also hunts through the "SOFTWAREMicrosoftWindowsCurrentVersionUninstall" registry key looking for security products to avoid.
it drops several more malware files signed with the pasted AMD digital signature to a directory it creates.
These files are stored within an 217kb encrypted cab file in the dropper's resources under the name "A". The cab file was encrypted and decrypted using a simple xor cipher with a rotating 16 byte key.
The cab file is decompressed and its contents are created on disk.
The code collects information about the system and xml formats this data prior to encryption for proper parsing.
Some of the malware uses an encrypted configuration file which is stored on disk as "racss.dat". This is encrypted by RC4.
It uses standard Win32 base cryptography functions to generate a CALG_RC4 session key to encrypt the collected data communications and POSTs it to the server.
Samples are usually signed with a fake certificate – we've seen two instances, one AMD and one Intel.
The attackers send commands and new modules to be executed to the victims through the C&Cs.
The C&C scripts store these temporarily until the victim next connects to retrieve local files.
BASE64 encoded.
RC4 encryption key.
And a set of "reporting" files, maintaining stolen system "info", error output, and "AgentInfo" output, from victim systems.
screenshot_task.dll is a 32-bit dll used to take a screenshot of the full desktop window and save it as a bitmap in %temp%.
It is used to create new processes, perform as a command line shell, and several other tasks.
Much like the prior office monkey "atiumdag.dll" component, this code collects identifying system information using standard win32 API calls.
It then uses the runtime resolved networking API calls to send the collected data back to a hardcoded c2 and set of urls.
